summary: DHH argues that the entrepreneurial world is too obsessed with exponential, hockey-stick growth and needs more companies that embrace modest, linear growth instead. He uses Basecamp's 14 years of steady growth as proof that sustainable businesses can thrive without venture capital or network effects. He contends that the obsession with exponential growth feeds monopolistic concentration by Facebook, Google, and others, which poisons markets and society. He calls for entrepreneurs to consciously choose linear growth as a sign of strength and independence, not weakness, and to make that choice early before their company's trajectory is locked in.
key_points: Linear growth is the natural result of good products sold at reasonable prices spreading through word of mouth, without artificial growth hormones from investors
The obsession with exponential growth creates monopolistic concentration that corrupts both business and society, as seen with Facebook and Google capturing all advertising value
The business press restricts entrepreneurial imagination by constantly asking who will be the next Google or Facebook instead of celebrating smaller sustainable businesses
The choice between exponential and linear growth is made early and locks a company's course — cap tables and investor demands are nearly impossible to undo later
Basecamp's 14 years of linear growth has served millions of users, employed fifty-odd people, and provided deep meaning to its founders
money_quotes: Exponential growth gets all the glory. Every startup story that lands on the cover of a magazine has a hockey-stick chart to flaunt.
If everyone wants to be the foundation, then there's nobody left to serve as the beams or cladding or tiles. That's a recipe for a concrete and corporate wasteland.
It's creative destruction without creative regeneration. More black hole, less forest-fire cleanse.
Capitalism as a system is prone to all manners of dysfunction, but few are as fatal as that of monopolies backed by exponential growth.
To eschew the exponential demands of investors is a sign of strength, not a mark of weakness. To be content with linear growth is a streak of independence, not absence of vision.
You can't move a tree by blowing at it softly once the roots are down. But you can radically change where a seed will land by doing the same.
Choosing to chase the exponential is just that, a choice. Which also means that choosing the linear is a choice too.
We are in dire need of such reprogramming of the entrepreneurial boot loader.
key_insight: Choosing modest, linear growth over exponential expansion is not a concession but a deliberate act of independence that produces more sustainable businesses and a healthier economy.
